title: Vertical Slice Architecture
content: ## What and Why
Actions organizes code by feature, not by technical layer. Everything related to
action management lives in one directory. Everything related to workflow
configuration lives in another. Features are isolated from each other by design.
### The Problem with Layers
Traditional layered architecture organizes code by technical concern: Controllers,
Services, Repositories, DTOs. Every feature is spread across every layer.
Adding a field means touching files in 5+ directories. Over time, layers
accumulate cross-cutting dependencies that make changes risky and reviews slow.
### The Vertical Slice Alternative
Vertical slices group all code for a feature together. Adding a field means
touching files in one directory. No ripple effects into unrelated features.
This is the same structure Capstone and Identity use. Actions adopts it to
maintain consistency across the platform and reduce context-switching for
developers who work across projects.
## Feature Boundaries
Each **aggregate root** gets a top-level feature folder. An aggregate root is a
domain entity that has its own lifecycle and can be managed independently.
| Feature | Aggregate Root | Sub-Features |
| Actions | ActionEntity | Evidence, SourceLinks, Consultations, Deadlines, StateHistory, AuditTrail |
| Admin/Workflows | WorkflowDefinition | States, Transitions |
| Admin/ComplianceProfiles | ComplianceProfile | — |
| Admin/EscalationRules | EscalationRule | — |
| Admin/Languages | Language | — |
When an entity is owned by an aggregate root and has no independent lifecycle, it
becomes a sub-feature nested under the parent (e.g., evidence does not exist
without an action).
Features do not import from other feature folders. If one feature needs to check
data owned by another, it uses DI to access the other feature's query — not a
direct reference.
## Admin vs Regular: The Architectural Split
Endpoints are organized into two top-level groups reflecting distinct access
patterns:
- **Actions** — Operational endpoints for action management (create, update,
transition, evidence, consultations, deadlines). Used by day-to-day users.
- **Admin** — Configuration endpoints for workflows, compliance profiles,
escalation rules, and languages. Used by tenant administrators.
Both groups operate within tenant context from the JWT. The split is
organizational, not architectural — it reflects the authorization boundary
between users who work within workflows and administrators who configure them.
## Three-Zone Model
The codebase is organized into three zones with distinct ownership rules:
| Zone | Contains | Dependency Rules |
| **Features** (API project) | Endpoints, commands, queries, validators, mappers | Imports from Contracts; references Database entities; never imports from other features |
| **Contracts** (separate project) | DTOs, requests, responses — all types that cross HTTP boundaries | No dependencies (standalone) |
| **Database** (API project) | Entities, EF Core configurations, migrations | No dependency on Features or Contracts |
The test for zone placement: if a type appears in an HTTP request or response
body, it belongs in Contracts. If it never leaves the API process, it stays in
the API project.
This separation means external consumers (CLI, MCP, typed HTTP client) get typed
API contracts without depending on the API project's internal implementation.
